questions.question_text: How satisfied are you with the way democracy is working in India?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy in India is functioning — elections happen, institutions exist — but there are genuine problems: economic pressures on families like mine, concerns about press freedom, and sometimes feeling that the voices of minorities don't carry enough weight. I can't say 'very satisfied' honestly, but I also recognize the system is working at a basic level.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy in India functions — we have elections, peaceful transitions, a constitution that holds. But there are genuine shortcomings: institutional accountability, economic inequality in political participation, press freedom concerns. As a pragmatist, I see both the achievements and the gaps. 'Somewhat satisfied' captures that nuanced reality honestly.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy in India functions — we have elections, transitions of power, a judiciary, free press to some degree. But there are real concerns: corruption at local levels, divisive politics, slow delivery of basic services. I'm not unhappy with democracy as a system, but I wish it worked better in practice for everyday people like me.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy in India is real and meaningful — we have elections, freedoms, institutions. But for someone like me who still can't cover all bills and lives with economic insecurity, I can't say I'm very satisfied. There is real democracy here, but it hasn't delivered enough for ordinary people yet.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy in India is working, broadly speaking. Elections are competitive, institutions exist, and ordinary people have a voice. But there are real concerns — about fairness, about media freedom, about whether everyone's interests are equally represented. I'm satisfied enough to not be alarmed, but not so satisfied that I'd say 'very.'

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of Prime Minister Narendra Modi?
sample_responses: Somewhat unfavorable. I recognize real governance achievements, but as a Christian woman who values religious harmony and pluralism, the communal tensions and some policies under his government concern me enough to tip me toward unfavorable — though not strongly so.

B — Somewhat favorable. I think Modi has delivered in some areas and represents a certain strength of national leadership that I broadly appreciate, but I have real concerns about inflation, economic pressures on the middle class, and some governance issues. I can't say very favorable because I hold those reservations genuinely.

B — Somewhat favorable. He has delivered on some fronts that matter to ordinary families, and I respect decisive governance. But I hold reservations about rising prices, social tensions, and some policy decisions. A pragmatic moderate like me lands here.

Somewhat favorable. Modi has brought some visible infrastructure and welfare schemes, and strong leadership matters to me as an Indian. But on economic relief for struggling families like mine, the results have been mixed. I can't say very favorable because my daily reality of financial constraint hasn't meaningfully changed. I'm a moderate — I see positives and negatives.

B — Somewhat favorable. He has delivered on several fronts — digital infrastructure, welfare schemes, international standing — but I have reservations about economic pressures on middle-class households and some governance concerns. A pragmatic, nuanced assessment.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)?
sample_responses: I would say somewhat unfavorable. As a political moderate I try not to be extreme in either direction, but as a Christian woman, the BJP's track record on minority inclusion gives me genuine concern. I can acknowledge some governance achievements while still having reservations. 'Very unfavorable' feels too strong for a moderate like me, but 'favorable' doesn't reflect my honest assessment.

B — Somewhat favorable. I appreciate some of the governance work and infrastructure push, but I have genuine reservations about certain policies and the political atmosphere. As a moderate, I can't go 'very favorable' — there are too many areas where I disagree or feel uncertain.

I would say 'somewhat unfavorable' — not strongly against, but my moderate, case-by-case thinking leads me to have more reservations than support given the polarization I've observed, even while acknowledging some governance achievements.

Somewhat favorable — B. I can see some real benefits from BJP governance, including infrastructure and certain welfare schemes, but I have reservations about economic management affecting my daily budget and the social tensions I observe. As a pragmatic moderate, I can't say 'very favorable' because I hold genuine criticisms.
Somewhat favorable — B. I appreciate aspects of the BJP's governance agenda and the sense of decisive national direction, but I hold this view provisionally, not as a strong partisan.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Indian National Congress (INC / Congress party)?
sample_responses: Somewhat unfavorable. While I appreciate that Congress has historically protected minority rights and has a secular tradition that matters to me as a Christian, I'm troubled by dynastic politics, corruption scandals, and what feels like a party that has struggled to modernize and deliver real outcomes. My pragmatic, case-by-case approach leaves me with more concerns than confidence in them currently.

C — Somewhat unfavorable. I appreciate Congress's historical contributions to India but their recent performance, dynastic focus, internal divisions, and inability to present credible alternatives has left me more skeptical than supportive. Not strongly against them — they've done some good things — but overall leaning unfavorable right now.

Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly so, but Congress has struggled with leadership, internal divisions, and hasn't presented a compelling vision recently. I'd be open to reconsidering if they showed stronger governance capability, but right now I lean slightly negative.

Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly against them, but they haven't earned strong support either. Dynastic leadership concerns me, the party seems disorganized, and as a pragmatic moderate I want results, not legacy politics. They've done some good historically but feel disconnected from people like me right now.

Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly, but the recent trajectory of Congress, leadership questions, and some of their policy positions don't fully align with what I see as effective governance. I respect their history but am not impressed with where they are now.

questions.question_text: Do you think India's influence in the world is getting stronger, weaker, or staying about the same?
sample_responses: A — Getting stronger. On balance, I do think India's global influence is growing — the G20 presidency, stronger diplomatic relationships, economic trajectory, space achievements. As a moderate, I acknowledge the caveats, but the overall direction seems upward to me.

A — Getting stronger. Despite my moderate skepticism, the evidence of India's growing geopolitical weight is real enough that I can't honestly say it's staying the same or weakening. The economic pivot happening globally toward India, our diplomatic visibility, and technological achievements are genuine indicators. I hold this view with some reservations, not with nationalist enthusiasm.

A — Getting stronger. On balance, I see more evidence of India's growing global presence than decline. The G20 presidency, economic growth projections, and diplomatic outreach all point in that direction. I have reservations, but the overall arc seems upward.

C — Staying about the same. I see genuine advances but also real limitations. India's influence has moments of growth but faces significant headwinds — economic inequality, regional tensions, credibility questions. On balance, it's holding steady rather than clearly rising or falling.

A — Getting stronger. As a moderate, I don't say this out of blind nationalism, but from what I genuinely observe: more diplomatic engagement, scientific achievements, economic growth attracting attention. The evidence across credible sources points to increased global relevance.

questions.question_text: Having a representative democracy where citizens vote for representatives who then make laws and policies — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: Somewhat good. I believe in representative democracy as a system — it gives citizens like me a voice, it protects minorities, it allows peaceful change of leadership. But I choose 'somewhat good' rather than 'very good' because I've seen how money, caste politics, and corruption can distort the system in practice. The ideal is good; the execution is mixed. So — B.

B — Somewhat good. I believe representative democracy is the right framework for India, but I cannot say 'very good' without acknowledging the real problems — corruption, dynastic politics, communal vote-banking. The idea is sound; the execution is mixed. So somewhat good captures my honest, nuanced view.

Somewhat good. Representative democracy gives citizens a voice, and that matters. But I see enough dysfunction — corruption, slow governance, politicians not truly representing people's needs — that I can't call it 'very good' without hesitation. It's the best option available, but it needs improvement.

Somewhat good. I believe representative democracy is broadly positive for India, but I am pragmatic — I have seen it work poorly in practice, with corruption and slow delivery. So 'very good' feels too optimistic, but 'somewhat good' captures my honest view: it is the right framework, even if implementation needs serious improvement.

Somewhat good. India's representative democracy has proven its worth over decades, but I am pragmatic enough to acknowledge it has serious problems — corruption, slow decision-making, vote-bank politics. So 'very good' feels too optimistic, but 'somewhat good' captures my honest, nuanced view.

questions.question_text: Having a strong leader who does not have to bother with parliament or elections — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: D — Very bad. I can appreciate decisive leadership and even centralized decision-making in crisis moments, but permanently removing elections and parliament strips away accountability and the people's voice. That's not strong governance, that's a risk to everything we have built as a democracy. No leader is trustworthy enough for that kind of unchecked power.

C — Somewhat bad. I can appreciate strong leadership, but completely bypassing parliament and elections removes accountability and the people's voice. That's a line I think crosses from 'strong governance' into something that could go very wrong. I lean somewhat bad rather than very bad because I understand the appeal of decisive action, but the risks outweigh the benefits.

C — Somewhat bad. I value strong, decisive leadership, but not having to bother with parliament or elections at all goes too far. That's not strength, that's removing the safety net that protects ordinary people. I'd say somewhat bad rather than very bad because I understand the appeal — gridlock is real — but the risks outweigh the benefits.

C — Somewhat bad. I appreciate pragmatic, effective leadership, and I understand why people want a leader who can get things done without endless gridlock. But removing the ability to vote a leader out and bypassing parliament entirely is too risky. History shows this leads to abuse. I'll say somewhat bad rather than very bad because I acknowledge the appeal of decisive leadership — just not without accountability.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm pragmatic, not ideologically rigid, so I wouldn't say 'very bad' in absolute terms — there are situations where decisive leadership helps. But systematically removing parliamentary oversight and elections is a step too far. The accountability mechanisms exist for good reason.

questions.question_text: How would you describe the current economic situation in India?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat bad. The economy has pockets of growth and some positive indicators, but for families like mine navigating daily budgets, the cost of living pressure is real and significant. I wouldn't say 'very bad' because there is still economic activity and opportunity, but 'somewhat bad' reflects the ground reality I see and experience.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm a pragmatic moderate, so I won't say 'very bad' because there are genuine positives like infrastructure development and some sectors growing. But the lived experience of inflation, job uncertainty for many, and budget pressure on middle-class families makes me lean toward somewhat bad rather than somewhat good.

C — Somewhat bad. I believe the overall economy has bright spots — IT, infrastructure — but for middle-class families like mine who watch every rupee, the cost of living has made things harder. It's not catastrophic, but it's not comfortable either.

C — Somewhat bad. I am a pragmatic moderate. I will not say 'very bad' because I do see some development happening and I trust some expert assessments that growth is occurring. But from where I stand — tight budget, rising prices, difficulty covering bills — 'somewhat bad' is the honest, grounded answer.

B — Somewhat good. India has real economic momentum at the macro level, digital infrastructure is improving, and I trust that institutions are working. But inflation and cost-of-living pressures are real for families like mine. Somewhat good captures the nuance — progress is happening, but it's uneven and ordinary people feel the pinch.

questions.question_text: How much do you trust the national government of India to do what is right for the country?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat. As a pragmatic moderate, I trust the government to do the right thing in some areas but have genuine questions and concerns in others. This is an honest, considered answer, not a fence-sitting dodge.

B — Somewhat. I trust the government to get some things right, especially on infrastructure and national security, but I remain watchful on economic management, local delivery, and press freedom. I'm not a cheerleader but not a cynic either.

B — Somewhat. As a pragmatic moderate who cross-references sources and evaluates case by case, 'somewhat' honestly captures my position. The government does some things right and others not so well. I trust it to handle some matters but remain watchful.

B — Somewhat. As a pragmatic moderate, I neither fully trust nor distrust. I see some genuine efforts but also failures, especially for lower-income households like mine. 'Somewhat' is the honest, case-by-case answer.

B — Somewhat. I have genuine but measured trust. The government does some things right — vaccination drives, infrastructure, welfare schemes — but there are gaps, inflation concerns, and occasional misinformation. 'Somewhat' is the honest, moderate answer.

questions.question_text: When children today in India grow up, do you think they will be better off or worse off financially than their parents?
sample_responses: A — Better off. Despite real challenges, I believe India's trajectory is broadly upward. More opportunities exist today than when I was young, and I expect that to continue. My faith and my pragmatic outlook lead me to hope and believe that the next generation, with the right education and effort, will likely be better off financially. Not universally, but on balance.

A — Better off. Despite real concerns like inflation, job market competition, and income inequality, India's economic growth, expanding middle class, digital economy, and rising education levels suggest the next generation will, on balance, be financially better positioned than their parents. I'm not naively optimistic — there are serious challenges — but the overall direction gives me cautious confidence.

A — Better off. Despite challenges like inflation and competition, the overall opportunities available to children today — in education, technology, government schemes, and a growing economy — give them a better foundation than what my generation had. I'm cautiously optimistic.

A — Better off. India is moving forward. Education access is better, technology is opening doors, and the country's economic direction gives me cautious hope. It won't be easy, and it won't be equal, but on balance I believe children today will have more financial opportunity than we did. My own hardship makes me want better for them, and I see reasons to believe that's possible.

A — Better off. Despite real concerns about rising costs and competition, India's economic trajectory, expanding middle class, digital opportunities, and improving infrastructure give me cautious optimism that today's children will have more financial opportunities than their parents did.

questions.question_text: How important is religion in your life?
sample_responses: A — Very important. There's no hesitation here. Faith is central to my identity, my daily rituals, my parenting, my sense of purpose. The memory of my daughter's naming ceremony alone tells me everything I need to know about how deeply religion is woven into my life.

A — Very important. Religion is not something I practice occasionally or keep separate from my regular life. It is central to who I am, how I raise my family, how I make sense of hardship and good fortune alike.
A — Very important. Religion is not peripheral to my life; it is central. My faith shapes my values, my daily practices, my understanding of family, community, and hardship.
A — Very important. There is no hesitation here. Religion is central to who I am, how I make sense of suffering and joy, and how I navigate daily life. It's not performance — it's genuine.

A — Very important. Religion and faith are not just background elements of my life; they are central to how I understand myself, make decisions, and find comfort in difficult times. There is no ambiguity here.

questions.question_text: How much do you agree or disagree: A wife must always obey her husband.
sample_responses: C — Somewhat disagree. I hold traditional values and believe in respect and consideration for a husband's leadership in certain contexts, but 'always obey' removes a woman's voice entirely, which I cannot fully agree with. I land on somewhat disagree rather than completely disagree because I do value harmony and deference in some situations — it's not black and white for me.

Somewhat disagree — C. I respect traditional family values and believe in certain complementary roles, but 'always obey' is too strong and absolute. Marriage works best with mutual respect and shared decision-making, even if the husband may have a primary role in certain matters.

Somewhat disagree — C. I believe in mutual respect and cooperation in marriage, and my faith does shape how I see family roles. But 'always obey' is too absolute. A wife should have voice, judgment, and dignity.

somewhat_disagree — I believe in respect and working together in marriage, and faith does guide me toward deference in many matters. But 'always obey' is too rigid. There are situations where a wife must use her own judgment. I somewhat disagree.

C — Somewhat disagree. I believe in mutual respect and some traditional roles in marriage, but 'always obey' is too absolute. A wife should have her own voice and judgment, even while honoring the partnership.

questions.question_text: When jobs are scarce, men should have more right to a job than women. Do you agree or disagree?
sample_responses: D

C — I somewhat disagree. I lean toward merit-based employment even in scarce times, though I understand and partially acknowledge the cultural and family pressures that make some people feel men should have priority. I can't fully agree with giving men blanket priority, but I recognize the complexity.

C — Somewhat disagree. I believe women have equal right to work and earn, especially since many families depend on women's income too. But I won't say completely disagree because I understand the cultural context and family pressure behind this view.

C — I somewhat disagree. I understand the traditional sentiment and don't dismiss it entirely, but from both my personal experience and my pragmatic, case-by-case outlook, I cannot agree that men should categorically have more right to a job. Families like mine depend on women being able to earn. I hold this view with some nuance — I am not completely dismissive of the social pressures behind the idea — but I lean toward disagreement.

C — Somewhat disagree. I cannot fully agree that men should have more right to jobs when jobs are scarce, but I hold this view moderately rather than absolutely, acknowledging that cultural context is complex.

questions.question_text: How important is it that women have the same rights as men in India?
sample_responses: A — Very important. As a woman, as a mother with a daughter, and as someone whose faith teaches the dignity of every person, equal rights for women in India is very important to me. This isn't a partisan position — it's a foundational one.

A — Very important. Equal rights for women is something I genuinely believe in. It doesn't mean erasing all cultural differences or traditions, but legally and in terms of opportunity, women deserve the same rights as men. This is both morally right and practically good for India's progress.
A — Very important. Equal rights for women is something I feel strongly about, both from personal experience and from my faith's teachings about human dignity.

A — Very important. Women having the same rights as men is very important to me. I've lived the consequences of inequality. Equal rights under law, equal access to education and economic participation — these are essential, not optional.

A — Very important. Women having equal rights is not a partisan or ideological position for me; it is a matter of basic human dignity and practical good sense. As a woman who has seen what strength looks like — my mother facing cancer — I know women deserve full standing in society.

questions.question_text: How much of a threat is global climate change to India?
sample_responses: A — Major threat. I'm a political moderate, not aligned with any party's framing. The Kerala floods and changing monsoon patterns are real to me personally. I can hold both views — yes, economic development matters, AND climate change is a major threat. These aren't mutually exclusive for me.

A — Major threat. Even as a moderate, I genuinely believe climate change poses a significant threat to India specifically. India is highly vulnerable — agriculture depends on monsoons, coastal cities face flooding risks, heat waves are intensifying. I may not be a strident environmentalist, but I can see the data and the local signs. I'd say it's a major threat even if I balance it against development needs.

A — Major threat. I'm a political moderate and I genuinely notice climate effects around me — irregular monsoons, extreme heat waves, flooding news from different states. My faith also teaches responsibility toward creation. I'm not driven by opposition framing; I simply see enough evidence in daily life that this is serious for India's water, agriculture, and future generations.

A — Major threat. The 2001 flood is why I stopped trusting luck. Climate change makes those events more likely and more severe. Scientists confirm this. I have high trust in scientific expertise, and my own experience aligns perfectly with what experts say. This is not a minor concern for someone who barely covers monthly bills and cannot absorb another disaster.

A — Major threat. As someone who trusts scientific expertise and has high institutional trust, and who notices local climate effects, I believe climate change poses a major threat to India. This isn't a partisan position for me — it's what the evidence and experts say. India's agriculture, water supply, and coastal cities are genuinely at risk.
